lessons.title: Superloop vs RTOS: When You Actually Need a Scheduler
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Imagine one cook running a whole kitchen alone, doing every step in strict order: chop, then stir, then plate, over and over. That is how the simplest tiny-computer programs work. But what if several dishes need attention at the same time and none can be left to burn? You need a smarter way to juggle. That is what this lesson is about.

**Build.** A *microcontroller* is a tiny computer on a single chip that runs one program. The simplest way to write that program is a **superloop** (also called a *bare-metal* program): you set things up once, then run one big loop forever. Each time around, the program does each job in turn — read a sensor, update a display, talk to wifi — then starts the loop again. This is easy to understand and uses very little memory.

The trouble starts when a device must juggle several jobs that each have their own timing. If one job is slow (say, sending data over a slow wifi link), every other job has to wait its turn — and a job that needed quick attention might miss its moment. You end up writing tangled code that tries to chop each job into tiny pieces by hand.

An **RTOS** (Real-Time Operating System) is a small helper program that solves this. "Real-time" means the device must respond by a *deadline*, reliably — not just eventually. With an RTOS you write each job as its own small, simple program called a **task**. A part of the RTOS called the **scheduler** rapidly switches the single CPU between the tasks, so they all *seem* to run at once — like a chef quickly moving between dishes so none burns.

**Recap.** Use a superloop when there is one job, or a few simple ones in order. Reach for an RTOS when a device must juggle several jobs at once and still meet deadlines. An RTOS costs a little extra memory and complexity, so do not add one just for show.

items.prompt: In the diagram, what is the scheduler's job in the RTOS (right) side?
choices: It runs all the tasks truly simultaneously on separate CPUs
It picks which task runs on the single CPU and switches between them quickly
It deletes tasks that are too slow
It replaces the need for a sensor
items.explanation: A small chip usually has one CPU, so only one task can actually run at a time. The scheduler's job is to pick which task gets the CPU and switch between them fast enough that they all seem to run at once. It does not run them truly in parallel, and it does not delete or replace hardware.

items.prompt: What best describes a 'superloop' (bare-metal) program?
choices: A program that sets things up once, then runs one big loop forever, doing each job in turn
A program that needs a desktop operating system like Windows to run
A program that can only blink a single LED
A program written in a special looping language
items.explanation: A superloop sets up the hardware once, then loops forever, handling each job in order each time around. It does not need a desktop OS (it runs directly on the chip), and it can do far more than blink one LED. The name describes the structure, not a language.

items.prompt: Superloop vs RTOS — when do you actually need an RTOS?
choices: Whenever your program is longer than ten lines
When a device must juggle several jobs at once and still meet timing deadlines, so a plain loop gets tangled
Only when you have more than one physical CPU
Whenever you want the code to look more advanced
items.explanation: An RTOS earns its keep when several independent jobs with their own timing no longer fit cleanly into one loop — the scheduler handles the juggling for you. Program length, looking advanced, or having multiple CPUs are not the deciding factors; a single CPU is the normal case where an RTOS helps most.

items.prompt: What does the 'real-time' in Real-Time Operating System mean?
choices: The system shows a clock on screen
The system runs as fast as physically possible at all times
The system must respond by a deadline, reliably and predictably
The system only works while connected to the internet
items.explanation: Real-time means reliable, predictable timing: the system must meet its deadlines, not merely finish eventually. It is about being on time, not about raw speed, a visible clock, or an internet connection.

items.prompt: In an RTOS, what is a 'task'?
choices: A to-do note for the programmer
One of the device's jobs written as its own small, self-contained program
A type of sensor
The single big loop in a bare-metal program
items.explanation: An RTOS lets you write each job (read the sensor, update the display, talk to wifi) as its own small program called a task. It is not a programmer's note or a sensor, and the whole point is that you no longer cram everything into one big loop.

items.prompt: Using the kitchen analogy, which pairing is correct?
choices: Superloop = a chef switching between many dishes; RTOS = one cook doing everything in strict order
Superloop = one cook doing everything in strict order; RTOS = a chef rapidly switching between dishes so none burns
Both are the same as a single cook
RTOS means hiring a second physical cook (CPU)
items.explanation: The superloop is like one cook doing every step in a fixed order. The RTOS scheduler is like a chef quickly switching between dishes so none burns — still one cook (one CPU), just clever switching. Hiring a second cook would be adding a second CPU, which is a different thing.

items.prompt: What is the typical sign that a superloop has been outgrown?
choices: The chip physically overheats
One slow job holds up the others, so a time-sensitive job misses its moment
The program file becomes too large to compile
The LED gets too bright
items.explanation: The classic warning sign is that a slow job (like a long wifi send) keeps the loop busy, so a job needing prompt attention is delayed and misses its deadline. This is a timing/structure problem, not overheating, file size, or LED brightness.

items.prompt: What is a real cost of adding an RTOS that means you should not add one needlessly?
choices: It always makes the program run slower than a superloop in every case
It uses extra memory and adds complexity (each task needs its own working space, plus new kinds of bugs)
It requires the chip to have a screen
It deletes your sensor data
items.explanation: An RTOS uses extra memory (each task needs its own stack), adds the scheduler's overhead, and introduces new kinds of bugs to watch for. That is why you avoid adding one just for show. It does not require a screen or destroy data, and it is not slower in every case.

items.prompt: Which job is perfectly fine to leave as a simple superloop?
choices: A device that simultaneously streams audio, runs a touchscreen UI, and keeps a network connection alive
A sensor that reads a value every second and sends it out, with nothing else going on
A robot coordinating motors, vision, and radio at once
A system that must answer three different deadlines at the same time
items.explanation: A single, simple, periodic job with nothing else competing for attention is textbook superloop territory — an RTOS would just add cost. The other choices involve several deadline-sensitive jobs at once, which is exactly where an RTOS helps.

lessons.title: Tasks, the Scheduler, and Switching Between Them
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A chef can only physically work on one dish at a time, yet a whole table of dishes comes out hot together. The trick is fast switching: a few seconds here, a few seconds there. An RTOS does the same with tasks on one CPU.

**Build.** Recall a **task** is one of the device's jobs, written as its own small program. At any instant the single CPU can only run *one* task. The **scheduler** is the part of the RTOS that decides which task runs right now and switches between them.

To keep track, the RTOS gives each task a *state*. A task is **Running** if it is on the CPU right now (only one at a time). It is **Ready** if it could run but is waiting its turn. It is **Blocked** if it is waiting for something — for example, data that has not arrived yet, or a fixed delay to finish; a blocked task uses no CPU at all, which is a big advantage over a loop that keeps checking. From the Ready group, the scheduler picks one to make Running.

When the scheduler moves the CPU from task A to task B, it must do a **context switch**. "Context" means everything the CPU was holding for task A — the numbers it was working on and the exact spot in the code. The RTOS *saves* all of that for A, then *restores* B's saved context so B continues exactly where it left off, as if it had never paused.

What makes the scheduler switch? A task can give up the CPU itself (for example, by asking to wait for data or to sleep for a while), or a small repeating timer called the **tick** — the RTOS's heartbeat — fires and gives the scheduler a chance to hand the CPU to another task.

**Recap.** One CPU, many tasks, each in a state (Running, Ready, or Blocked). The scheduler picks the next task; a context switch saves one task and restores another so the switch is invisible to the code.

items.prompt: In the timeline diagram, why do the tasks appear to all run 'at the same time'?
choices: Each task secretly has its own CPU
The CPU runs each task for a short turn, then switches, fast enough that they all seem active at once
The tasks are merged into one big program before running
Only the fastest task ever actually runs
items.explanation: The diagram shows the single CPU giving each task a short turn, then switching to the next. Because the turns are short and rapid, the tasks appear to run together even though only one runs at any instant. They are not given separate CPUs, not merged, and none is starved out.

items.prompt: Looking at the task-state diagram, which statement is true?
choices: Many tasks can be in the Running state at the same time on one CPU
Only one task is Running at a time; others wait in Ready or Blocked
A Blocked task is constantly checking and so uses lots of CPU
A Ready task is one that has finished forever
items.explanation: On one CPU exactly one task is Running; the rest are Ready (could run, waiting their turn) or Blocked (waiting for an event or timeout). A Blocked task uses no CPU — that is the benefit of blocking over busy-checking — and Ready does not mean finished.

items.prompt: What is the scheduler's main responsibility?
choices: To compile your program
To decide which task gets the CPU next and switch between tasks
To store the device's data permanently
To draw the user interface
items.explanation: The scheduler chooses which Ready task runs next and performs the switches. It is not the compiler, not storage, and not a UI — it is purely the part that hands the single CPU around among tasks.

items.prompt: On a chip with one CPU, how many tasks can be in the Running state at the same time?
choices: As many as you create
Exactly one
Two: one foreground and one background
None — tasks never actually run
items.explanation: One CPU can execute exactly one task at any instant, so only one task is Running at a time. The others are Ready or Blocked. Creating many tasks does not give you many CPUs.

items.prompt: A task asks to wait until new sensor data arrives. What state does it enter, and what is the benefit?
choices: Running; it keeps the CPU so it reacts instantly
Blocked; it gives up the CPU and uses no CPU time while waiting, so other tasks can run
Ready; it spins in a tight loop checking for data
It is deleted and re-created when data arrives
items.explanation: Waiting for data puts the task in the Blocked state, where it uses no CPU at all — the scheduler can run other tasks meanwhile and wake this one when data arrives. Busy-spinning while Ready would waste CPU, and the task is not deleted.

items.prompt: In 'context switch,' what does the word context refer to?
choices: The comments the programmer wrote
Everything the CPU was holding for a task: its working numbers and the exact spot in the code
The wiring diagram of the chip
The name the programmer gave the task
items.explanation: Context is the CPU's working state for a task — the values it was using and the precise place in the code it had reached. Saving and restoring this is what lets a task resume as if it never paused. It is not comments, wiring, or a name.

items.prompt: When the scheduler switches the CPU from task A to task B, what must it do?
choices: Throw away task A entirely and start task B from the beginning
Save task A's context, then restore task B's saved context so B continues where it left off
Run both A and B at once on the same CPU
Ask the user which task to keep
items.explanation: A context switch saves A's working state and restores B's previously saved state, so B resumes exactly where it had paused. A is not discarded (it resumes later), the two do not run at once on one CPU, and no user input is involved.

items.prompt: What is the RTOS 'tick'?
choices: A bug that makes the program stutter
A small repeating timer — the RTOS heartbeat — that regularly gives the scheduler a chance to switch tasks and measure delays
The sound the chip makes
A one-time event at startup only
items.explanation: The tick is a periodic timer interrupt that acts as the RTOS heartbeat: each tick lets the scheduler check whether to switch tasks and helps count down delays. It is not a bug, a sound, or a one-time event.

items.prompt: Which of these can cause the scheduler to switch tasks?
choices: A task voluntarily asking to wait (for data or a delay), or the tick timer firing
Renaming a variable in the source code
Adding a comment to the program
Nothing — once a task starts it always runs to the very end
items.explanation: Switches happen when a task gives up the CPU (waiting for data or sleeping) or when the periodic tick gives the scheduler a chance to act. Renaming variables and comments are just source code and have no runtime effect, and tasks do not have to run to completion before another can run.

lessons.title: Priorities, Preemption, and Sharing Data Safely
lessons.content:
**Hook.** In a busy kitchen, some things cannot wait: if a pot is boiling over, the chef drops the salad and deals with it *now*. An RTOS works the same way — some tasks are more urgent than others, and an urgent one can interrupt a less urgent one.

**Build.** Each task is given a **priority** — a number saying how urgent it is. The scheduler follows a simple rule called **preemptive priority scheduling**: the most urgent task that is Ready always runs. If a more urgent task becomes Ready while a less urgent one is running, the scheduler *immediately* takes the CPU away from the less urgent task and gives it to the urgent one. This interrupting is called **preemption**. If two tasks have the *same* priority, the scheduler can share the CPU between them by giving each a short turn in rotation — this is **time slicing**.

Now, tasks often need to share information, and doing this carelessly causes corruption. Three tools help:

- A **queue** is like a mailbox line: one task drops a copy of a message in one end, another task takes it out the other end. It is the safe, normal way to pass data between tasks.
- A **semaphore** is a simple signal: one task (or a hardware event) raises a flag to say "something happened," and a waiting task notices and acts. It is how you wake a task when an event occurs.
- A **mutex** (short for *mutual exclusion*) is a single key. Before a task touches a shared item, it must take the key; it returns the key when done. Because there is only one key, just one task changes the item at a time, so the data never gets half-updated by two tasks at once.

*Why protect shared data at all?* If two tasks update the same value at the same time, one can overwrite the other midway, leaving a garbled result. The mutex's one-key rule prevents this.

**Special case — hardware events.** A *hardware event* (like a button press) is handled by a short piece of code called an *interrupt handler*. Such code must never wait, so it cannot use the normal blocking calls. Instead it uses a special "from-interrupt" version (often described as "give from ISR," where ISR means Interrupt Service Routine) to *signal* a waiting task to do the real work.

**Recap.** Priorities plus preemption make urgent work happen first; time slicing shares the CPU among equals. Queues pass data, semaphores signal events, and a mutex's single key keeps shared data from being corrupted.

items.prompt: In the queue diagram, how does data get from the producer task to the consumer task?
choices: Both tasks edit the same variable at the same time
The producer puts a copy into one end of the queue; the consumer takes it from the other end
The consumer reaches into the producer's memory directly
The scheduler emails it
items.explanation: A queue works like a mailbox line: the producer drops a copy in one end and the consumer takes it from the other. Because a copy is passed, the two tasks never have to poke at the same memory at once, which is what makes it safe. Editing one shared variable simultaneously is exactly the unsafe pattern queues avoid.

items.prompt: What does a task's 'priority' represent?
choices: How much memory it uses
How urgent it is — used by the scheduler to decide who runs first
The order it was written in the source file
Its color in the debugger
items.explanation: Priority is a measure of urgency that the scheduler uses to pick which Ready task runs. It is not about memory use, source order, or any display color.

items.prompt: A low-urgency task is running when a high-urgency task becomes Ready. Under preemptive priority scheduling, what happens?
choices: The high-urgency task waits politely until the low one finishes
The scheduler immediately takes the CPU from the low task and runs the high task
Both run at half speed
The high task is dropped because the CPU is busy
items.explanation: Preemption means a more urgent Ready task interrupts a less urgent running one right away — that is the whole point. The urgent task does not wait its turn, the CPU does not split (one CPU runs one task), and the urgent task is certainly not dropped.

items.prompt: What does 'time slicing' do?
choices: Permanently picks one task and ignores the rest
Shares the CPU among tasks of equal priority by giving each a short turn in rotation
Cuts the program into separate files
Slows the CPU clock to save power
items.explanation: Time slicing fairly rotates the CPU among equal-priority tasks, giving each a short turn so none is starved. It does not pick a single permanent winner, split source files, or change the clock speed.

items.prompt: What is a queue used for in an RTOS?
choices: Locking a shared resource so only one task uses it
Passing messages/data from one task to another by copy, like a mailbox line
Measuring how long a task runs
Giving a task a higher priority
items.explanation: A queue carries copies of messages from one task to another in first-in-first-out order — the safe, normal way to hand data across tasks. Locking a resource is a mutex's job, and a queue neither measures time nor changes priority.

items.prompt: What is a mutex, and why protect shared data with it?
choices: A faster kind of CPU; it makes shared data load quicker
A single 'key' a task must hold before touching a shared item, so only one task changes it at a time and the data is never left half-updated
A message line that copies data between tasks
A timer that wakes a task after a delay
items.explanation: A mutex (mutual exclusion) is a single key: a task must take it before touching shared data and return it after. Because only one key exists, only one task edits the data at a time, so two tasks can't garble it by writing at once. A message line is a queue, and a delay timer is unrelated; a mutex is not hardware.

items.prompt: Why is it dangerous for two tasks to update the same value at the same time without protection?
choices: It uses too much electricity
One task can overwrite the other partway through, leaving a garbled, half-updated value
It always crashes the compiler
The chip forgets the value forever
items.explanation: Updating a value isn't instant, so two unprotected tasks can interleave and one overwrites the other midway, producing a corrupted result. A mutex prevents this by allowing only one updater at a time. It is not about electricity, the compiler, or permanent memory loss.

items.prompt: What is a semaphore most commonly used for?
choices: Copying large data buffers between tasks
Signaling — one task or event raises a flag, and a waiting task notices and acts
Compiling the program faster
Giving every task the same priority
items.explanation: A semaphore is a simple signal: it says 'something happened,' waking a waiting task to respond. Copying bulk data is what a queue does; a semaphore does not compile code or set priorities.

items.prompt: A button press is handled by a short interrupt handler that must never wait. How should it hand the real work to a task?
choices: By doing all the work inside the handler and blocking until it finishes
By using a special 'from-interrupt' signal (give from ISR) to wake a waiting task that does the real work
By renaming the task to make it run
By turning off the scheduler
items.explanation: Interrupt handlers must stay short and never block, so they use the special 'from-ISR' signal to wake a normal task that does the heavier work. Doing it all in the handler (and blocking) is exactly what's forbidden; renaming tasks or disabling the scheduler does nothing useful here.

items.prompt: You want to send a stream of sensor readings from one task to another. Which tool fits best?
choices: A mutex, because it passes data
A queue, which carries each reading as a message from producer to consumer
A priority change, which moves the data
Nothing — just let both tasks write the same variable
items.explanation: A queue is built for handing a stream of messages from a producer task to a consumer task safely. A mutex only locks shared data (it doesn't transport messages), changing priority doesn't move data, and letting both write one variable is the unsafe pattern queues exist to avoid.

lessons.title: Priority Inversion, Task Stacks, and the RTOS Landscape
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Picture an urgent task stuck waiting on a key that a slow, low-urgency task is holding — and the slow task keeps getting shoved aside by other work, so it never returns the key. The urgent task waits and waits. This famous trap is called *priority inversion*, and it once kept resetting a robot on Mars.

**Build.** Set up three tasks: **L** (low urgency), **M** (medium), and **H** (high). Task L takes a mutex (the single key) to use a shared item. Then H wakes up and wants that same key, so H has to wait for L — reasonable so far. The trouble: a medium task M becomes Ready. Because M is more urgent than L, the scheduler runs M instead of L. So L never gets the CPU to finish and return the key, which means H stays stuck — effectively M, a *less* urgent task than H, is now delaying H. That backwards situation is **priority inversion**.

The common fix is **priority inheritance**: while H is waiting on the key that L holds, the RTOS *temporarily* raises L to H's urgency. Now M cannot push L aside; L quickly finishes, returns the key, and drops back down, so H proceeds. (Enabling exactly this fix is what rescued NASA's Mars Pathfinder lander in 1997.)

**Task stacks.** Each task needs its own private scratch space in memory called a **stack**, used for its function calls and local values. You must give each task *enough* stack. On these tiny chips there is usually no safety net: if a task uses more than its stack allows, it quietly overruns into other memory and causes mysterious corruption — a **stack overflow**. The cure is to size each stack generously and turn on the RTOS's stack-overflow checking.

**The landscape.** **FreeRTOS** is a small, popular RTOS you simply include in your program — minimal and everywhere. **Zephyr** is a much bigger system: a full RTOS plus drivers, a way to describe the hardware (a *device tree*), a build-configuration menu, and networking — more like a small Linux. There are also modern approaches in the *Rust* language: **RTIC** organizes tasks around hardware events with the schedule worked out before the program even runs, and **Embassy** uses *async* tasks (jobs that pause and resume at marked points) sharing a single stack to save memory. The big idea for all of them is the same: write each job separately and let the system juggle them.

**Recap.** Priority inversion is a high task stuck behind a low one holding a needed key while a medium task hogs the CPU; priority inheritance fixes it. Give every task enough stack and enable overflow checks. FreeRTOS is the small classic, Zephyr the larger Linux-like option, with Rust's RTIC and Embassy as modern alternatives.

items.prompt: In the priority-inversion timeline, what causes high task H to be delayed in the 'without inheritance' scenario?
choices: H has the lowest priority of all
Medium task M keeps running instead of low task L, so L never finishes and returns the key H needs
The CPU has overheated
H forgot to ask for the key
items.explanation: H is blocked waiting for the key (mutex) that L holds. Because M outranks L, M runs instead of L, so L can't finish and release the key — leaving H stuck behind a less urgent task. H is actually the highest priority; the delay is the inversion, not overheating, and H did request the key.

items.prompt: Which scenario is 'priority inversion'?
choices: A high-urgency task runs before a low-urgency one — exactly as intended
A high-urgency task is stuck waiting on a key held by a low task, while a medium task keeps the low task from finishing
Two tasks swap their priority numbers in the code
A task runs faster than expected
items.explanation: Priority inversion is when a high task is effectively delayed by a medium task, because the low task holding the needed key can't get CPU time to release it. The high task running first is normal (not inversion), and it isn't about editing priority numbers or running fast.

items.prompt: In the classic inversion setup, what role does the medium task M play?
choices: It holds the key that H wants
It is more urgent than L, so it keeps running and prevents L from finishing and releasing the key
It is the highest-priority task and should run
It has nothing to do with the problem
items.explanation: M is the troublemaker: being more urgent than L, it gets the CPU instead of L, so L can't release the key that H needs. M does not hold the key (L does), and M is not the highest priority — H is. M is central to the problem.

items.prompt: How does 'priority inheritance' fix priority inversion?
choices: It permanently makes every task high priority
While the high task waits, it temporarily raises the low task (holding the key) to the high task's urgency, so the medium task can't push it aside
It deletes the medium task
It removes the mutex so no key is needed
items.explanation: Priority inheritance temporarily boosts the key-holding low task up to the waiting high task's urgency. Now the medium task can't preempt it, so it finishes and releases the key quickly, then drops back down. It does not permanently change priorities, delete tasks, or remove the lock.

items.prompt: What is the well-known real-world example of priority inversion mentioned here?
choices: A phone battery that drained too fast
NASA's Mars Pathfinder lander, which kept resetting until priority inheritance was enabled
A car engine that stalled
A laptop that overheated
items.explanation: The famous case is NASA's 1997 Mars Pathfinder, where priority inversion repeatedly triggered resets until engineers enabled priority inheritance. The other examples are unrelated to inversion.

items.prompt: What is a task's 'stack'?
choices: A pile of finished tasks waiting to be deleted
The task's own private scratch memory for its function calls and local values
The list of all tasks in priority order
The chip's main power supply
items.explanation: Each task gets its own stack: private working memory for its function calls and local variables. It is not a pile of dead tasks, the scheduler's priority list, or anything to do with power.

items.prompt: What is a 'stack overflow' on a small microcontroller, and why is it dangerous?
choices: The task runs too fast; this just wastes power
A task uses more stack than it was given and overruns into other memory, causing silent corruption because there is usually no safety net
Two tasks are given the same name
The display shows too many lines
items.explanation: If a task needs more stack space than allotted, it spills into neighboring memory, corrupting it — and tiny chips usually lack hardware to catch this, so failures are mysterious. The fix is to size stacks generously and enable overflow checking. It is not about speed, names, or display lines.

items.prompt: Which best captures the difference between FreeRTOS and Zephyr?
choices: FreeRTOS is a full Linux-like system; Zephyr is just a tiny scheduler
FreeRTOS is a small RTOS you include in your program; Zephyr is a much larger system with drivers, hardware description (device tree), build configuration, and networking — more like a small Linux
They are the same product with different names
Zephyr can't switch between tasks; FreeRTOS can
items.explanation: FreeRTOS is a minimal RTOS you simply link into your app, while Zephyr is a far bigger, Linux-like platform with drivers, a device tree, configuration menus, and networking. The first choice reverses them, and both can certainly schedule tasks.

items.prompt: What are RTIC and Embassy?
choices: Two brands of microcontroller chips
Modern approaches in the Rust language for organizing concurrent embedded tasks (RTIC around hardware events; Embassy using async tasks on a shared stack)
Replacements for the CPU's stack
Two kinds of sensors
items.explanation: RTIC and Embassy are modern Rust-based ways to structure embedded concurrency: RTIC arranges tasks around hardware events with a schedule fixed before runtime, and Embassy uses async tasks sharing one stack to save memory. They are not chips, sensors, or stack replacements.

items.prompt: A high-urgency task occasionally misses its deadline, but only when a medium task is busy, and it shares a mutex with a low task. Most likely cause and fix?
choices: The stack is too small; make every stack tiny
Priority inversion; use a mutex with priority inheritance so the low key-holder can't be pushed aside
The tick is too fast; turn the RTOS off
The display is too slow; remove the screen
items.explanation: The symptom — a high task delayed specifically when a medium task runs, while a low task holds the shared key — is textbook priority inversion. Enabling priority inheritance on that mutex lets the low holder finish and release the key. Shrinking stacks, disabling the RTOS, or removing a screen address none of this.
